name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
أَرَءَيْتَ ٱلَّذِى يُكَذِّبُ بِٱلدِّينِ (١)
```

```english
Did you see him who denies the Retribution? (1)
```

```arabic
فَذَٰلِكَ ٱلَّذِى يَدُعُّ ٱلْيَتِيمَ (٢)
```

```english
That is the one, who drives away the orphan, (2)
```

```arabic
وَلَا يَحُضُّ عَلَىٰ طَعَامِ ٱلْمِسْكِينِ (٣)
```

```english
and does not urge the feeding of the needy. (3)
```

```arabic
فَوَيْلٌ لِّلْمُصَلِّينَ (٤)
```

```english
Woe to those who pray (4)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَن صَلَاتِهِمْ سَاهُونَ (٥)
```

```english
but are heedless of their prayers (5)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ يُرَآءُونَ (٦)
```

```english
—who show off (6)
```

```arabic
وَيَمْنَعُونَ ٱلْمَاعُونَ (٧)
```

```english
but deny aid. (7)
```

## (Commentary)

This is a threat (وعيد) directed at those who, while professing the religion (المنتحلين بالدين), adopt the character of the hypocrites (المنافقين), such as being heedless of prayer (السهو عن الصلاة), showing off in deeds (الرياء في الأعمال), and withholding basic necessities (منع الماعون)—all of which are inconsistent with a sincere belief in the Recompense (التصديق بالجزاء).

The *surah* could potentially be either Meccan or Medinan, and it has been said that half of it is Meccan and the other half is Medinan.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي يُكَذِّبُ بِالدِّينِ** **"Have you seen him who denies the Retribution?"** (107:1). The "seeing" (الرؤية) here could refer to visual perception or it could mean knowledge (المعرفة). The address is to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله as a listener, and thus it is directed toward every listener. What is meant by "the religion" (الدين) is the Recompense (الجزاء) on the Day of Judgment; thus, the one who denies the religion is the one who rejects the Resurrection (المعاد). It has also been said that it refers to religion in the sense of the creed (الملة).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَذلِكَ الَّذِي يَدُعُّ الْيَتِيمَ** **"That is the one who drives away the orphan,"** (107:2). The word *da’* (الدع) means to repel with violence and harshness. The letter *fa* (الفاء) in "that is" (فذلك) implies a conditional meaning; the underlying sense is: "Have you seen the one who denies the Recompense? If so, you can recognize him by the traits that necessarily follow from his denial. If you do not know him, then he is the one who repels the orphan with violence and treats him harshly, fearing not the consequence of his evil deed. Had he not denied the Recompense, he would have feared it, and had he feared it, he would have shown him mercy."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلا يَحُضُّ عَلى طَعامِ الْمِسْكِينِ** **"and does not urge the feeding of the needy."** (107:3). The word *hadd* (الحض) means to encourage or urge (الترغيب). The speech assumes a suppressed genitive (مضاف); that is, he does not urge people toward the feeding of the food of the needy. It has been said that the use of the word "food" (طعام) rather than "feeding" (إطعام) is to intimate that the needy person is, as it were, the owner of what is given to him, as in His saying, the Exalted: **وَفِي أَمْوالِهِمْ حَقٌّ لِلسَّائِلِ وَالْمَحْرُومِ** **"and in whose wealth there is a right for the beggar and the deprived."** (51:19). It has also been said that "food" in this verse means "feeding."

The expression uses "urging" (الحض) rather than "feeding" because urging is more general than practical urging, which is realized through the act of feeding itself.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَوَيْلٌ لِلْمُصَلِّينَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنْ صَلاتِهِمْ ساهُونَ** **"Woe to them who pray—those who are heedless of their prayers,"** (107:4-5). That is, they are oblivious (غافلون), showing no concern for it and being indifferent if they miss it entirely, or miss it at certain times, or delay it beyond its time of merit (وقت فضيلتها), and so on.

In this verse, there is an application of the description "he who denies the religion" to these people who pray, due to the presence of the *fa* of derivation (فاء التفريع). This indicates that they are not free from hypocrisy (نفاق), for they deny the religion through their actions while they outwardly profess faith.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الَّذِينَ هُمْ يُراؤُنَ** **"those who show off,"** (107:6). That is, they perform acts of worship to be seen by people; they work for the sake of people rather than for Allah, the Exalted.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيَمْنَعُونَ الْماعُونَ** **"and deny [giving] basic necessities."** (107:7). The term *al-ma’un* (الماعون) refers to everything that assists another in meeting a life necessity, such as a loan (القرض) you provide, a favor (المعروف) you perform, or household items (متاع البيت) you lend. The various specific interpretations mentioned in the words of the commentators all trace back to this general meaning.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **أَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي يُكَذِّبُ بِالدِّينِ** (Have you seen him who denies the Retribution?), he said: "It was revealed concerning Abu Jahl and the disbelievers of Quraysh." Regarding His saying: **الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنْ صَلاتِهِمْ ساهُونَ** (those who are heedless of their prayers), he said: "It means those who abandon it, for every human being may be distracted (يسهو) within the prayer." Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام said: "It is the delaying of the prayer from its earliest time without an excuse."

In *al-Khisal*, narrated from ‘Ali عليه‌السلام in the "Hadith of the Four Hundred," he said: "There is no deed more beloved to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, than prayer. Therefore, let nothing of worldly affairs distract you from its times, for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, censured certain people saying: **الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنْ صَلاتِهِمْ ساهُونَ** (those who are heedless of their prayers), meaning they are oblivious (غافلون) and treat its times with contempt."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration to Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl, who said: "I asked the Righteous Servant (Abdu Salih) عليه‌السلام about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنْ صَلاتِهِمْ ساهُونَ** (those who are heedless of their prayers). He said: 'It is neglect (التضييع).'"

**I say:** There are other narrations with these same meanings.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Bayhaqi in his *Sunan* narrated from ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib regarding **الَّذِينَ هُمْ يُراؤُنَ** (those who show off), that he said: "They show off with their prayer."

In the same source, Abu Nu’aym, al-Daylami, and Ibn ‘Asakir narrated from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding His saying **وَيَمْنَعُونَ الْماعُونَ** (and deny [giving] basic necessities), that he said: "It is what people assist one another with: the axe, the pot, the bucket, and the like."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration to Abu Basir from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام in a tradition, he said: "And His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَيَمْنَعُونَ الْماعُونَ** (and deny [giving] basic necessities) refers to the loan (القرض) you provide, the favor (المعروف) you perform, and the household items (متاع البيت) you lend, and among it is the alms-tax (الزكاة)."

**I say:** The interpretation of *al-ma’un* as the alms-tax (الزكاة) is also narrated through Sunni channels from ‘Ali عليه‌السلام as found in *al-Durr al-Manthur*, with the wording: "*Al-ma’un* is the obligatory alms-tax (الزكاة المفروضة)—they show off with their prayers and withhold their alms-tax."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Qani’ narrated from ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib, who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saying: 'A Muslim is the brother of a Muslim; when he meets him, he greets him with peace (السلام), and he returns to him what is better than it. He does not withhold the *ma’un*.' I asked: 'O Messenger of Allah, what is the *ma’un*?' He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله replied: 'Stone, iron, water, and the like.'"

**I say:** In another narration, he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله interpreted "iron" as copper pots and the iron of the axe, and "stone" as stone pots.
